titleStmt.title: The Lone Woman and Last Indians Digital Archive

availability.p: Periodicals and books published before 1 January 1923 are in the public domain. If re-publishing, quoting from, or reproducing images from this digital archive, we request that you cite it as follows: Lone Woman and Last Indians Digital Archive, Center for Digital Humanities, University of South Carolina.

Periodicals and books published on or after 1 January 1923 may be protected by U.S. Copyright Law. All attempts to secure permission for republishing post-1923 material in this archive have been made. The names, logos, and identifying images, as well as the content of the articles, remain the property of the periodicals and/or of the authors and artists themselves. Further reproduction, as well as quotation beyond fair use, is prohibited; permission must be obtained from the copyright holder. Archival material published in this archive is used with permission of the institution or library that holds it. Further reproduction, as well as quotation beyond fair use, is prohibited; permission must be obtained from the copyright holder.
Responsibility for any use of the contents of this digital archive rests exclusively with the user.

bibl.title: The Lost Woman of San Nicolas

rs.persName: THE LOST WOMAN OF SAN NICOLAS

p.span: It was now getting late and we returned on board with the determination of making the next day a thorough exploration of the upper portion of the island. Accordingly, after breakfast next morning, we started with all of our men except the cook. Reaching the low, sand flat before mentioned Brown and the four men stretched out in a line and crossed to the other side of the island, while I continued along near the shore, on the same side I had come, towards the head of the island. Brown and his men made no discoveries in passing over the island. He then sent the men back to search along the border of the sand flat and among the bushes where the basket had been found. He went up towards the head on that side until he struck the track he had seen the night before. He followed it up again until he found a short piece of drift-wood. From this he concluded she had been down to the beach for firewood and had dropped this piece on her way. From this point he saw further up the ridge three huts. Upon reaching them he found them made of whale ribs, covered with brush, although they were open on all sides. The grass was quite high within them, showing that no one had occupied them for some time. He was now on one of the highest parts of the ridge, and he began to look about in all directions. The sandy flat was in plain sight and he could see most of the men. At last his eye caught sight of a small black object a long distance off that seemed to be moving. It looked at first very much like a crow. Walking towards it he soon found that it was the Indian woman. She was seated in an inclosure similar to those already described, so that her head and shoulders were barely visible above it. As he approached her two or three dogs, like those we had seen before, close to her, began to growl. Without looking in the direction of Brown she gave a yell and the dogs disappeared. Brown had halted within a few yards of her, and once began to signal to the men by placing a hat on the ramrod of his gun and raising and lowering it. He soon succeeded in attracting their attention and they came towards him. In the meantime Brown had an opportunity of observing the woman. She was seated, cross-legged, upon some grass which covered the enclosure on the inside and which no doubt served as a bed. Her only dress was a kind of gown, leaving her neck and shoulders bare, and long enough, when she stood up to reach her ankles. It was made of shag skins, cut in squares and sewed together, the feathers being pointed downward. Her head had no cover save a thick mass of matted hair of a yellowish-brown color, probably from the exposure to the sun and weather, and which looked as if it might have rotted off. She was engaged in stripping the blubber from a piece of seal skin which she held across one knee, using in the operation a rude knife made of a piece of iron hoop.

span.span: Within the enclosure was a smoldering fire and without a large pile of ashes, and another of bones, which would indicate that this had been her abode for a long time. From the time Brown first arrived within hearing distance she kept up a continual talking to herself, occasionally shading her eyes with her hands and gazing steadily at the men who were seen walking around on the flat below. She was evidently much interested in their movements. As the men came near Brown motioned them spread out so as to prevent her escape if she was so disposed. Just before the men reached her camp Brown, who had not been seen by her, came around in front.

div.p: To his great surprise she received them with much dignity and politeness, bowing and smiling with ease and self-possession. As fast as the men came up she greeted each in the same way. The men seated themselves on the ground around, the woman all the time talking, although not a word of hers could be understood, although our Indians spoke several dialects. From a sack or bag made of grass she took some roots, known among the Californians as the Carcomites, and another root whose name I did not learn, and placed them in the fire. When they were roasted she offered them to us to eat. We found them very palatable, indeed. We were now desirous of taking her on board the schooner. We did not apprehend that she would try to escape, as she seemed much pleased with our company. We commenced making signs for her to go with us, but she seemed unable to comprehend them until we intimated that she must gather up all her food, when she set about the work with the greatest alacrity, and commenced putting them in a large basket, such as in general use among Indians of the coast. She had considerable dried blubber of the seal and sea elephant. This was all carefully collected. There was also a seal's head, in such a decayed condition that the brains were oozing out. As her desire this also was taken along.

She seemed desirous of preserving everything that would sustain life, thus indicating the sad experience of her eighteen years of solitude. When all was ready she took a burning stick in one hand or on her shoulders. She trotted along merrily, leading us to a spring of good water, which came out under a shelving rock near the beach. Here we found a store of bones in a cleft of the rocks. It would seem that in time of scarcity she would come here and suck the bones as long as any nutriment could be obtained from them. Here also were pieces of dried blubber hung on stakes, above the reaches of the foxes and dogs which inhabited the island. We gained the woman's confidence by taking care to preserve all these articles. On the way to the schooner she led us past another spring, which she seemed to have used for bathing, as she stopped and washed her hands and face. This spring was not far from the landing. When we reached the boat we made motion for her to step in, which she did, kneeling down in the bow, holding to the sides with her hands. When we got on the vessel she sought the vicinity of the stove, keeping as near to it as possible, which act indicated more of her bitter experience on the island.

We offered her some of our food, which she ate with relish; in fact, from this time she seemed to prefer our style of food to her own. Brown went to work that afternoon and made her a skirt, or a petticoat out of some bedticking, with which she was much pleased, continually calling out attention to it. This skirt, with a man's shirt and neck-tie, constituted her new wardrobe. While Brown was sewing she made signs that she wanted to sew, and Brown gave her a needle and thread. After this was done by one of us she knew how to use it. I gave her an old cloak, or heavy cape, which was much torn and dilapidated. She patiently sewed up all the rents and made it quite serviceable in the cold, windy weather which prevailed occasionally. In sewing she thrust the needle into the cloth with her right hand and pulled it through, drawing the thread tightly with her left hand.

The following day we moved on shore and made a camp on a narrow piece of ground between the beach and the rocks, and made a shelter by leaning some poles against the rocks and covering them with sail cloth. We made a similar shelter for her at a short distance, covering it with brush. We remained on the island hunting otter about a month. During the time she evinced no disposition to leave, but was generally talking, singing and wandering about the island. She assisted in the camp work, bringing wood and water when it was needed. Her vessels for carrying water were quite unique. They were woven of grass, shaped somewhat like a demijohn, except that they had wider mouths, and were lined with a thin coating of asphaltum. The process of lining them was rather ingenious. She put several pieces of asphaltum, which is found in great quantities along the beach, in the bottom of the basket, or receptacle, and then on top of them some hot pebbles. When the asphaltum was melted by the pebbles, by a quick, rotary motion, she would cover the inside of the receptacle with an even coating, after which the surplus, with pebbles, were thrown out. These baskets were water-tight and would last a long time. She had several of these water vessels in process of construction when we found her. She would work at one a few minutes, abandon it, and try another. I am not aware that she ever completed one when with us. When we killed otter we usually, after skinning them, threw the bodies in to the sea. One day we killed a large female, which was with young. When about to cast it into the sea, as usual, she in her mute way, protested. The young one, which was nearly grown and covered with fur, was taken out and the skin stuffed and made to look quite natural, by one of the party. She took a great fancy to the baby otter, and suspended it to a pole of her shelter, would swing it back and forth for hours, talking to it in a kind of sing-song tone.

After hunting successfully about a month we put everything on the schooner and sailed for Santa Barbara. Not long after sailing a furious gale arose, which threatened to engulf the little vessel. The woman made signs that she would allay the wind, and kneeling down, facing the quarter whence the wind came she began making incantations, or prayers, which she continued for some time, and at intervals during the storm. When the wind abated she pointed with triumph to the clear sky, as much as to say: "See what I did.["] We approached the shore early in the morning. It was evident that she had never seen it before, or any of the ordinary sights of a settlement. An ox team, with a Spanish cart, passed on the sand. It is not known whether it was pleasure or wonder which passed through her mind. The yoke which tied the animals to each other and to the cart; the uncouth wheels, with their rotary motion, which she imitated with various gestures, were inexpressibly wonderful, delightful and ludicrous. She laughed, talked, gesticulated all at once. After our landing a horseman, among others, came to the beach. This was a new creature, also, but she had the courage to examine it, touching horse and man in succession. She turned to her friends, for so may be considered her captors, and straddled the first two fingers of her right hand over her left thumb, and imitating the galloping of a horse with her finger, gave shouts of delight. Ashore she was taken to Nidever's house and put in care of his wife.

The story soon spread that the lost woman of San Nicolas was found. The possibility of there being a woman alive alone on a desert island in the ocean, with only wild animals for companions, had been discussed in many households, and who was now in the midst of such warm-hearted people was a subject of intense interest. As the years had passed and nothing was heard of her the general conclusion was that she had perished, probably devoured by the wild things on the island. The fathers of the Mission had exerted themselves in the matter and had offered a reward of $200 dollars for information that would lead to her discovery. When Nidever had reported finding tracks on the island, and other evidences of life, Father Gonzales had confidently asserted that she was alive, and her coming back to the mainland and life at last aroused the greatest interest everywhere the news penetrated.

Hundreds flocked the Nidever's house. Among others came Fathers Gonzales, Sanchez and Jimeno. Though familiar with all the dialects of the Indians, of the coast not a word of her language could be understood. Indians from Santa Ynez, Los Angeles and other places were brought, with no better success; not one of them understood a word of her language. She soon became very expert in conversing by signs, however, and continued to tell portions of her story, so that but little uncertainty attended the narrative. She related that when she went back after her child she wondered a long time without finding it; then she concluded that the dogs had eaten the child and she laid down a long time and became sick, and could not eat anything and got around again and began to eat. She had often seen vessels on the sea, but none of them ever came to take her away. She finally became reconciled to her fate and commenced the routine of life which was to be varied only by hunger, thirst, cold and fear of wild animals for near a score of years.

She was received with the utmost kindness. Almost everyone made her a present of money, clothing, or trinkets, which, however, she would immediately give to her friends or to the children who came to see her. In all those days the Panama steamers used to touch at Santa Barbara, and always all the passengers were desirous of seeing the lost woman. She would often put on her finest dress of feathers and go through the movements which the people termed dancing through it had little resemblance to the graceful dances of the ballroom. She became very much attached to the family, which feeling was mutual, for Nidever several times refused large sums which offered to have her exhibited to the public of San Francisco.

She was thought to be about 50 years of age at the time she was found. As near as could be made out by her signs she had, at the time of being left on the island, two children, one of which was a nursing babe, the other some years older, though in the opinion of some, the older child had died some years previously. She had a smooth face, though the skin on her body was much wrinkled. It was but a short time before her death that they succeeded in making her understand their desire to have some words of her own language. The following are about all that were learned of it: a hide she called "tocah"; man, "nache"; the sky, "toygwah"; the body, "puoo-chay."

She was like a child in every respect as to her appetite, having no control whatever. She was exclusively fond of fruit, which she would eat at all hazards. It produced a dysentery, which in spite of careful nursing and attendance, terminated fatally in about four weeks. During her sickness it was thought that a diet of seal's meat, such as she had been accustomed to, would relieve her. Some was procured and roasted, but she shook her head and laughed, and rubbed her fingers along her worn-out teeth, signifying that they were too old. She was buried by the Mission fathers and most of her island trinkets and her shag feather dresses were sent to Rome and found in a place in the Vatican museum.

p.span: In conclusion, we can but question ourselves—What kept this lone woman's heart warm through all her long years of solitude, with no companions but those wild Russian dogs? When found at last she was as blythe and sunny as a child, with a heart still as warm as that of any child, with love and gratitude for her deliverance at last, and again, why that other, her best dress? Why was it? We will ask ourselves these things, perhaps, but who can answer to the mysteries in the realms of woman['s] nature?
